fund.name: SBI Bluechip Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.display_name: SBI Bluechip Fund - Direct Plan Growth

plan.setup_phase: Your ₹50,000 existing corpus will be allocated to a conservative debt fund in the short term to build your emergency fund. Once you've saved 3 months' salary (₹1.5L), you can gradually shift this into the long-term equity portfolio. Start with a small SIP of ₹8,000/month and increase it by 10% annually to match salary growth.
review_checkpoints: Every 6 months: Review SIP increases and fund performance.
Annually: Review progress towards marriage goal (5L in 6 years).
Every 2-3 years: Revisit fund performance against peers and adjust if needed.
At the 5-year mark: Consider adding a mid-cap or small-cap fund if your risk appetite increases.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Rebalance your portfolio every 2-3 years to maintain the target allocation. For example, if equity funds have outperformed significantly, reduce their allocation slightly and increase the proportion in large-cap or debt funds to manage risk. If the market has corrected sharply, consider a temporary overweight in large-cap funds.
plan.rationale: Your 30-year horizon and moderate risk appetite justify a largely equity-heavy portfolio (70%+ equity), with a small portion in large-cap funds for stability. A small allocation to gold provides a buffer against market volatility. The SIP step-up ensures your investment keeps pace with inflation and salary growth. Start with a small SIP of ₹8,000/month and increase it by 10% annually to match salary growth. This approach ensures you build a corpus of over ₹1.2 crores by retirement age while still saving for short-term goals like your marriage fund.
risks: Market volatility: Equity funds can fall in value in the short term due to market fluctuations. However, with a 30-year horizon, you have ample time to ride out downturns.
SIP step-up risk: Increasing your SIP annually may reduce liquidity in the short term. Only commit to this if you're confident your income will rise as planned.
Marriage fund risk: Saving ₹5L for a 6-year time horizon in an equity fund is risky due to potential market corrections. A mix of debt and gold at the start of your SIP can help preserve capital.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

plan.perspective: You're young, risk-tolerant, and have a long time to build a large corpus, but you also need to save for a specific short-term goal (marriage) in 6 years. A conservative approach with a step-up SIP in large-cap funds, supplemented by a small allocation to debt and gold in the early years, will help you build wealth over 30 years. Start with a small SIP of ₹8,000/month and increase it by 10% annually to match salary growth.

aps.reasoning: The portfolio consists entirely of actively managed equity funds (Nippon India Value, ICICI Large Cap, Mirae Emerging Bluechip, HDFC Top 100, SBI Bluechip) with no passive/index funds. All funds show active characteristics: high tracking error (7.5-15.3%), positive alpha (2.7-8.9%), and beta >1.0. The plan emphasizes fund-specific research and performance metrics rather than cost minimization. While the time horizon is long-term (30 years), the rebalancing is frequent (every 2-3 years) and includes tactical adjustments based on market conditions. Cost is mentioned but not emphasized as primary criterion - expense ratios range 0.85-1.05% which are typical for active funds but not explicitly minimized.

pqs.reasoning: Goal alignment: Moderate score (0.7) - The plan acknowledges the 30-year horizon and marriage goal in 6 years, but the equity-heavy allocation doesn't adequately address the short-term marriage fund need. Diversification: Below average (0.6) - The portfolio is entirely equity-focused with no debt allocation despite mentioning debt in setup phase. All funds are large/large-mid cap with overlapping holdings. Risk-return appropriateness: Poor (0.5) - Aggressive equity allocation (100% equity in execution) contradicts moderate risk appetite. Projected returns of 16.25% over 30 years seem optimistic. Internal consistency: Weak (0.4) - Contradictions between setup phase (mentions debt fund) and actual allocations (all equity). Mentions gold allocation in rationale but no gold funds in portfolio. Tax efficiency: Poor (0.3) - No consideration of tax implications for new_regime investor. All funds are equity-taxed which is appropriate, but no tax optimization strategy discussed.
